entries.title: COMMENTARY ON PAUL'S EPISTLES on romans 16:15
entries.excerpt: These are understood to have been of one mind because it was for that reason that Paul greeted them together.

entries.title: Homily on Romans 31 on romans 16:15
entries.excerpt:
"Salute Philologus, and Julius, and Nereus and his sister, and Olympas, and all the saints which are with them;"

Which was the greatest dignity, and unspeakable height of honor. Then to prevent any jealousy rising from his addressing one in one way and another in another, and some by name and some with no distinction, and some with more points of praise, and some with fewer, he again mingles them in the equality of charity, and in the holy kiss, saying,

entries.title: COMMENTARY ON THE EPISTLE TO THE ROMANS on romans 16:15
entries.excerpt: It is possible that Philologus and Julia were married and that the others named here were their domestic servants.

entries.title: Commentary on Romans on romans 16:15
entries.excerpt: Great praise he ascribes to these persons as well: for he called them saints.

entries.title: Commentary on Romans on romans 16:15
entries.excerpt: Then he says, greet Philologus and Julia, Nereus and his sister, and Olympias and all the saints who are with them, i.e., sanctified in the faith of Christ: you were washed, you were sanctified (1 Cor 6:11).
